discussion_comments.comment_body:
does it introduce a new mechanism, why not use `dictPrefetchKeys`?
even for mset command, we may also need to access old value (key size histgram)

If the remaining key number is less than 2*MGET_BATCH, we call prefetch them once, skip this ugly optimization:(, so we can only have a single code path. actually we have similar mechanism in memory_prefetch.c
Addressed in [a46e18965](https://github.com/redis/redis/pull/15133/commits/a46e189655611453c92750f8583a0193a29c3bae)
Fixed in 718ae4c5c — the fast path now checks `server.allow_access_expired` before skipping expired fields, matching `hashTypeNext()` and `hashTypeLength()` behavior.
Fixed in 718ae4c5c — added `skip_expired = !server.allow_access_expired` flag so the expiration filter mirrors `hashTypeNext()` behavior. Also removed the dead `batch_de` array.
I assume it is optimized. Yet, maybe it worth to try benchmark, say, keys_stack[64].

Good call. Running a benchmark sweep on dedicated experimental branches with keys_stack[64], [128], and [256] across the scan-* benchmarks on both x86 and ARM. Will post results before picking the final size.

Ran the sweep on 3 branches (`keys_stack[64]`, `[128]`, `[256]`) targeting `memtier_benchmark-1Mkeys-generic-scan-.*` (11 tests) on `x86-aws-m7i.metal-24xl` and `arm-aws-m8g.metal-24xl`, n=1 each.

Pairwise vs the 256 baseline:

| Arch | Comparison | Regressions >3% |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| x86 | 64 vs 256 | count-500-pipeline-10 **-6.5%**, count-10-incremental -3.1% |
| x86 | 128 vs 256 | scan-pipeline-10 **-5.3%**, count-500-pipeline-10 -4.5% |
| ARM | 64 vs 256 | scan-pipeline-10 -3.6%, count-500-pipeline-10 -3.5% |
| ARM | 128 vs 256 | scan-pipeline-10 **-11.2%**, type-pipeline-10 -7.1%, cursor-count-500-pipeline-10 -3.2% |

Pattern is consistent across both archs: the heavy-throughput tests (`*-pipeline-10`, `count-500-*`) regress when the stack shrinks — smaller stack spills to heap earlier and forces extra `vecPush` doublings on the hot path. 256 (~2 KiB on the stack) never loses on any test.

Keeping `keys_stack[256]`.

i also 256 is a bit big, since we already know `count` number, so we can allocate the specific size, avoiding reallocate+memcpy during running.
```
vecReserve(&keys, count);
```

@ShooterIT, I don’t think this is a good idea. A user could set it to a very large value (e.g., UINT_MAX) to retrieve all items, which could lead to disproportionately large memory allocation. The value 256 optimize the common case.

> a very large value (e.g., UINT_MAX)

I was thinking we can avoid necessary `reallocate+memcpy` during running. It is easy to avoid the bad case, like `vecReserve(&keys, min(count, 1024));`

and didn't you ask test 64 size?

I suggested testing 64 because I wanted to validate whether 256 was large enough to start hurting locality/cache behavior in the common case, which I expect is usually only tens of elements. The benchmark sweep answered that: shrinking the stack buffer regressed the hot throughput cases, while 256 didn’t lose.

Regarding `vecReserve(&keys, min(count, 1024));` , it complicates the logic more than it should (unless some benchmark will clearly shows otherwise).

Partially applied in bc8c714a4: when `count > 256` (stack buffer), the keys vec is pre-reserved to `count` — single allocation, skipping the 256→512→…→count doubling path. Kept on the stack for the common small-COUNT case. @moticless raised a UINT_MAX concern on the ungated form (see sibling comment); the 256 gate sidesteps the common case but does not cap the upper bound — open to wrapping with `min(count, <cap>)` if we want a hard ceiling.

Agreed — the ungated `vecReserve(&keys, count)` exposes a cheap DoS vector (user passes a huge COUNT → disproportionate allocation). In bc8c714a4 the pre-reserve only fires when `count > 256` (the stack buffer size), which keeps the common case on the stack. I have not added an explicit upper cap yet. If you think we should add one, `min(count, 1M)` or similar would bound the worst case at ~8 MiB of pointer storage — happy to add if you prefer.

Noted. Current state (bc8c714a4) already reserves only when `count > 256`, so the grow-by-doubling you wanted to avoid is skipped in the large-COUNT case. @moticless called the explicit `min(count, 1024)` wrapper "complicates logic more than it should" — I agree unless a benchmark shows otherwise. Leaving as-is.
Thanks for the sanity check — aligned with keeping `keys_stack[256]`. I updated the approach notes with the sweep data and the rationale.

i am getting confusing. current code still exposes DoS, right?
```
    if ((size_t)count > sizeof(keys_stack) / sizeof(keys_stack[0]))
        vecReserve(&keys, count);
```
if we use a huge count, the allocation will be huge too.
```
HSCAN hash 0 count 10000000000000
1) "0"
2) 1) "a"
   2) "b"
   3) "c"
   4) "d"
info memory
# Memory
used_memory:1509648
used_memory_human:1.44M
used_memory_rss:5472256
used_memory_rss_human:5.22M
used_memory_peak:80000001484512
used_memory_peak_human:72.76T
```
if we want to avoid the attack, we should set a limit, like `vecReserve(&keys, min(count, 1k/1m));`

besides, this check `if ((size_t)count > sizeof(keys_stack) / sizeof(keys_stack[0]))` is not necessary, since vecReserve has similar check inside. 

if we don't worry about `reallocate+memcpy` during scanning, we should remove them.
I really think we should avoid vecReserve() in here. We took care to the common case. If count is very large the bottleneck probably won't be the growth of the vector.

both are okay to me.
@filipecosta90  if you want to avoid dynamic growth, keep `vecReserve` with a limit, if not, please delete this code block directly. maybe you can run benchmark for them

Is there a way to make these two use the same code?
the same below.

Collapsed in 1b8bcce6a. Both the MSETNX existence-check pass and the write pass now use a single batched-loop structure; `use_prefetch` only gates the prefetch call (not the loop shape), and the `else { simple loop }` branches are gone. The prefetch itself is routed through a new `msetMaybePrefetchBatch` helper that re-fetches the slot dict per batch (also addressing the augmentcode/cursor bugbot concern about stale `dict *`).

Fixed in 1b8bcce6a. The slot dict is now re-fetched per batch via the new `msetMaybePrefetchBatch` helper, so a prior batch's `setKey` → `expireIfNeeded` → `freeDictIfNeeded` → `dbAddByLink` sequence (cluster mode, `KVSTORE_FREE_EMPTY_DICTS`) can't leave us with a dangling pointer. Cost: one extra `kvstoreGetDict` call per 16 keys.

Added a regression test in `tests/unit/type/string.tcl` ("MSET overwrites expired keys across batch boundary") that exercises the expire-then-insert path across a 20-key MSET (two batches). ASAN + `test-sanitizer-address` and `test-external-cluster` are green on the follow-up.

why not apply this optimization for HSCAN/SSCAN?

for the array, maybe it can be void* [] type, do i miss something?
addressed in https://github.com/redis/redis/pull/14148/commits/69d9296d6d84a30a2f416f08fe6662de5ff57351

For the doubling reallocation strategy - have you considered the memory impact when COUNT values are very large (e.g., 10,000+)? Would a more conservative growth strategy (like 1.5x) be worth exploring to reduce memory spikes?
